  • Development machine: macOS, Linux, or Windows server
  • Network: Wi-Fi or cellular data

Server-Side Setup

Section titled “Server-Side Setup”

Mosh uses a zero desktop installation approach - just run mosh-server on the server:

iOS App Configuration

Section titled “iOS App Configuration”
  1. Download Moshi from the App Store
  2. Add SSH host:
    • Host address: your server IP or domain
    • Port: SSH port (default 22)
    • Username: your server username
  3. Configure authentication:
    • Face ID protection
    • Key or password authentication

Connecting to Your AI Agent

Section titled “Connecting to Your AI Agent”

Recommended Use Cases

Section titled “Recommended Use Cases”

1. Remote AI Agent Server

Section titled “1. Remote AI Agent Server”

Set up your Mac as an always-on AI agent server:

  • Mac sits on the desk running AI agents in tmux
  • When you leave, agents keep working
  • Push notification on your iPhone when input is needed
  • Approve continuation from anywhere

2. Coffee Shop Coding

Section titled “2. Coffee Shop Coding”
  • Start long-running tasks at the coffee shop
  • Continue your daily routine
  • Review results after receiving completion notification
  • No need to stare at progress bars

3. Multi-Device Home Workflow

Section titled “3. Multi-Device Home Workflow”
  • Desktop for heavy development
  • iPhone to monitor and respond to agent requests
  • Review code changes from the couch
  • Final deployment checks from bed

4. Staying Productive While Traveling

Section titled “4. Staying Productive While Traveling”
  • Stay connected at hotels, airports, trains
  • Network switches without disconnection
  • Emergency fixes without opening a laptop
  • True mobile development experience

Best Practices

Section titled “Best Practices”

Session Management

Section titled “Session Management”

Security Recommendations

Section titled “Security Recommendations”
  1. Always use SSH key authentication
  2. Enable Face ID protection
  3. Use VPN or Tailscale for an extra security layer
  4. Regularly update mosh-server

Network Optimization

Section titled “Network Optimization”
  • Prefer mosh over ssh in unstable network environments
  • Configure appropriate UDP port ranges
  • Use Tailscale for NAT hole-punching

Comparison with Alternatives

Section titled “Comparison with Alternatives”

Moshi vs Other iOS Terminal Apps

Section titled “Moshi vs Other iOS Terminal Apps”
FeatureMoshiBlink ShellTermiusPrompt 3
Mosh ProtocolNativeNoNoNo
Push NotificationsYesNoYesNo
Voice InputYesNoNoNo
Face IDYesYesYesYes
Agent OptimizedYesPartialNoNo
PriceFree during betaSubscriptionSubscriptionOne-time purchase

Unique Advantages

Section titled “Unique Advantages”
  1. Designed for AI coding agents - Deep integration with AI agent workflows
  2. Resilient connections - True mobile experience, unafraid of network switches
  3. Push notifications - Know instantly when tasks complete, no constant monitoring
  4. Voice control - Hands-free coding experience
  5. Zero desktop installation - No extra software needed on your dev machine

Limitations and Notes

Section titled “Limitations and Notes”

Current Limitations

Section titled “Current Limitations”
  • iOS only - No Android version yet
  • Free during beta - Some premium features may be paid after launch
  • Requires mosh-server - Must be installed on the server

Known Issues

Section titled “Known Issues”
  • Special character input: Some Unicode characters may require keyboard switching on third-party keyboards
  • Long output scrolling: Output exceeding 10,000 lines may experience scroll lag on older iPhones
  • tmux shortcuts: Complex multi-pane operations (Ctrl+Arrow combos) may require long-press on some keyboard layouts

Pricing

Section titled “Pricing”

Completely free during beta - All features are available.

After official launch, basic features will remain free. Some advanced features (like team collaboration, advanced push notification rules) may require a paid subscription.

Troubleshooting and FAQ

Section titled “Troubleshooting and FAQ”

Connection Issues

Section titled “Connection Issues”

Q: Can’t connect to server, getting “Connection refused”

A: Check the following:

  1. Confirm mosh-server is installed: which mosh-server
  2. Check if firewall allows UDP ports 60000-61000
  3. Try connecting with regular SSH first to verify network reachability

Q: Connection drops frequently

A:

  1. Check server-side mosh-server version (1.4+ recommended)
  2. Confirm network environment isn’t blocking UDP traffic
  3. Try establishing a Tailscale VPN first, then connect

Configuration Issues

Section titled “Configuration Issues”

Q: Face ID verification fails

A:

  1. Check if Face ID is enabled in iOS settings
  2. Re-add SSH keys in settings
  3. Ensure device isn’t in Focus mode blocking biometrics

Q: Keys not responding

A:

  1. Check if “Extended Keyboard” is enabled
  2. Try shaking the device to access undo/redo panel
  3. Restart the app to reinitialize keyboard state

Performance Issues

Section titled “Performance Issues”

Q: Output display is laggy

A:

  1. Reduce terminal scroll history lines
  2. Use escape-time 0 in tmux to reduce input delay
  3. Disable unnecessary highlighting and syntax coloring

Related Articles

Section titled “Related Articles”
  • Best iOS Terminal Apps of 2026: AI Coding Agent Comparison
  • How to Use Claude Code on iPhone: Best Remote Coding Setup
  • Using Mac as a Remote Endless Work Agent
  • Blink Shell Alternatives 2026: What to Use After the Subscription Change

Technical Architecture

Section titled “Technical Architecture”

Mosh Protocol

Section titled “Mosh Protocol”

Mosh (mobile shell) is a remote terminal application that supports:

  • Intermittent connectivity
  • Roaming capability (maintains connection when IP changes)
  • Intelligent local echo and line editing
  • More robust and responsive than SSH

System Architecture

Section titled “System Architecture”

Security Architecture

Section titled “Security Architecture”
  • SSH keys stored in iOS Keychain
  • Face ID biometric unlock
  • Keys never leave the device
  • End-to-end encrypted connection

References

Section titled “References”
  • Official Website: https://getmoshi.app
  • Mosh Protocol: https://mosh.org - Open-source mobile terminal protocol
  • App Store: Search “Moshi - Mobile Terminal”
  • Documentation and Tutorials: https://getmoshi.app/articles

Summary

Section titled “Summary”

Moshi delivers a true mobile development experience for AI coding agents through these core advantages:

  1. Resilient connections - Mosh protocol ensures sessions survive network switches and app backgrounding
  2. Push notifications - Proactive alerts when agents need input, no constant monitoring needed
  3. Voice control - Local Whisper recognition for hands-free command input
  4. Secure and reliable - Keychain key storage + Face ID protection

Who it’s for: Developers who need to remotely monitor AI agents and want to stay productive while on the move.

Quick start: brew install mosh -> Download Moshi App -> Connect to your development machine.

Tags:
  • iOS Terminal
  • AI Coding Agent
  • Claude Code
  • Remote Development
  • Mosh Protocol
  • Developer Tools

Terminal Tunnel: Share Your Terminal Anywhere with tty-share

Jan 21, 2026
Vibe Sparking

Terminal Tunnel: Share Your Terminal Anywhere with tty-share

Section titled “Terminal Tunnel: Share Your Terminal Anywhere with tty-share”

Ever needed to share your terminal session with a colleague for pair programming, remote debugging, or collaborative troubleshooting? tty-share is a beautifully simple tool that lets you share your Linux or macOS terminal over the Internet with zero setup required for the remote participant.

Features and Core Highlights

Section titled “Features and Core Highlights”

tty-share is a lightweight, open-source terminal sharing solution that stands out for its simplicity:

Why Choose tty-share?

Section titled “Why Choose tty-share?”
  • Zero Remote Setup: The person joining needs no tool installation—they can join directly from their browser
  • Single Binary: Written in Go with no dependencies—works on Linux, macOS, even Raspberry Pi
  • Open Source: Fully transparent codebase available on GitHub
  • Secure Connections: TLS encrypted communication between your machine and the relay server
  • Flexible Sharing: Share via public Internet or local network only
  • Read-Only Mode: Optionally create read-only sessions for demonstration purposes

How It Works

Section titled “How It Works”

When you share your terminal over the Internet (outside your NAT), tty-share connects to a proxy server that mediates communication between participants. The official server runs at tty-share.com, but you can also host your own for complete control.

Installation and Configuration

Section titled “Installation and Configuration”

macOS (Recommended)

Section titled “macOS (Recommended)”

The easiest installation method on macOS is using Homebrew:

Other Platforms

Section titled “Other Platforms”

For Linux, Windows (WSL), or other platforms, download the latest binary release from the GitHub releases page.

Build from Source

Section titled “Build from Source”

If you have Go installed:

For cross-compilation (e.g., building for Raspberry Pi):

Usage and Best Practices

Section titled “Usage and Best Practices”

Basic Usage - Share Your Terminal

Section titled “Basic Usage - Share Your Terminal”
  1. Start a sharing session:

You’ll receive output like:

  1. Share the URL with your colleague

  2. They join by opening the URL in their browser OR using:

Read-Only Sessions

Section titled “Read-Only Sessions”

For demonstrations where you don’t want remote input:

Local Network Only

Section titled “Local Network Only”

To share only within your local network:

TCP Port Forwarding

Section titled “TCP Port Forwarding”

Similar to SSH’s -L option, you can create TCP tunnels:

This makes tty-share listen locally on port 1234 and forward connections to example.com:4567 from the remote side (requires server -A flag).

Recommended Scenarios for Work and Life

Section titled “Recommended Scenarios for Work and Life”

Pair Programming Sessions

Section titled “Pair Programming Sessions”

tty-share excels at real-time collaborative coding:

  1. Host starts a session with tty-share --public
  2. Share the URL with your pair programming partner
  3. Both can see and interact with the same terminal
  4. Perfect for debugging, code reviews, or teaching

Remote Debugging

Section titled “Remote Debugging”

When troubleshooting server issues:

  • Share the terminal session showing logs or error states
  • Get immediate visual feedback from team members
  • No need for screen sharing software

Teaching and Demos

Section titled “Teaching and Demos”

For educational content:

  • Use --readonly flag so viewers can’t interfere
  • Great for live coding demonstrations
  • Students can follow along in real-time

Cross-Platform Collaboration

Section titled “Cross-Platform Collaboration”

Works seamlessly when:

  • One person is on macOS, another on Linux
  • Remote participant doesn’t have SSH configured
  • Joining from a smartphone or tablet (via browser)

Comparison with Similar Tools

Section titled “Comparison with Similar Tools”

vs VS Code Live Share

Section titled “vs VS Code Live Share”
Featuretty-shareVS Code Live Share
Remote SetupNone (browser)Install VS Code
AuthenticationNone requiredGitHub/MS account
Terminal OnlyYesAlso file editing
Resource UsageMinimalHeavy

vs tmate.io

Section titled “vs tmate.io”
Featuretty-sharetmate.io
Browser JoinYesRecently added
Binary SizeSingle static binaryMultiple dependencies
ARM SupportNative cross-compileRequires specific distros

vs gotty/webtty

Section titled “vs gotty/webtty”
Featuretty-sharegotty/webtty
WebRTCOptionalWebTTY uses it
SimplicityOne commandSetup required
Relay ServerPublic + self-hostSelf-host only

Security Considerations

Section titled “Security Considerations”

Encryption

Section titled “Encryption”
  • TLS connection from your machine to the relay server
  • HTTPS for browser participants
  • Similar security model to tmate, VS Code Live Share

End-to-End Encryption

Section titled “End-to-End Encryption”

Currently on the TODO list. If you don’t trust the public relay server, you can:

  1. Host your own relay server (see GitHub repo)
  2. Use local network-only sharing
  3. Combine with VPN for additional security

Best Practices

Section titled “Best Practices”
  • Use --readonly for public demos
  • Don’t share sensitive credentials in shared sessions
  • Consider self-hosting for sensitive work
  • Be mindful of what commands you run while sharing

Troubleshooting and FAQ

Section titled “Troubleshooting and FAQ”

Session won’t start

Section titled “Session won’t start”

Problem: Port already in use

Solution: Specify a custom port:

Remote participant can’t connect

Section titled “Remote participant can’t connect”

Problem: Firewall blocking connection

Solution: Try local network mode or check firewall settings

Connection drops

Section titled “Connection drops”

Problem: Network instability

Solution: Simply restart the session and share the new URL

Can’t join from browser

Section titled “Can’t join from browser”

Problem: Outdated browser

Solution: Update to Chrome 90+, Firefox 88+, Safari 14+, or Edge 90+

Reference Links

Section titled “Reference Links”
  • Official Website: https://tty-share.com
  • GitHub Repository: https://github.com/elisescu/tty-share
  • Installation Guide: https://tty-share.com/install
  • Releases: https://github.com/elisescu/tty-share/releases

Summary

Section titled “Summary”

tty-share fills an important niche in the developer toolkit—simple, instant terminal sharing without the friction of account setup, software installation, or complex configuration. Whether you’re pair programming, remote debugging, or demonstrating commands to a team, tty-share gets the job done with minimal overhead.

Key Takeaway: When you need to share your terminal right now without explaining how to install SSH or set up VS Code, tty-share is the answer.

Tags:
  • tools
  • terminal
  • pair-programming
  • remote-work
  • developer-tools

The Complete Guide to Vibe Coding That's Actually Useful

Jan 21, 2026
Vibe Sparking

The Complete Guide to Vibe Coding That’s Actually Useful

Section titled “The Complete Guide to Vibe Coding That’s Actually Useful”

Three months ago, I spent 15 minutes building my pregnant wife a “Tinder for restaurants” app.

She was deep in first-trimester food aversions. Nothing sounded good. Choosing dinner had become a 45-minute negotiation that ended with both of us frustrated and hungry.

The app shows her a restaurant. She swipes right if it sounds appealing, left if not. After a few swipes, we have a shortlist. Decision made.

I’ve been writing code professionally for years. I could have built this the “right” way—proper architecture, tests, CI/CD pipeline. It would have taken two weeks and I never would have started.

Instead, I described what I wanted, let the AI generate the code, tweaked what needed tweaking, and deployed.

Total time: 15 minutes.

Total lines I wrote by hand: 0.

That’s vibe coding. Not abandoning your judgment. Strategically choosing when to apply it.

The Mental Shift

Section titled “The Mental Shift”

Most people use vibe coding wrong.

They watch a demo of someone building a “Spotify clone” in 20 minutes, get excited, try to build “the next big app,” hit a wall at 15 components, and conclude the whole thing is overhyped.

That’s backwards.

Vibe coding is about matching effort to stakes.

Some code deserves careful architecture. Production systems. Anything with real users. Code that will be maintained for years.

Other code doesn’t. Internal tools. One-off scripts. Prototypes that test ideas before you invest real engineering time. Personal utilities only you will ever use.

We’ve always known this intellectually. The problem is that “quick and dirty” still takes hours when you’re writing everything by hand. So we either over-engineer throwaway code or don’t build it at all.

Vibe coding changes the economics. The stuff that doesn’t deserve careful architecture now takes minutes instead of hours. You get the tool without the investment.

  • Before: “Is this tool worth 8 hours of my time?” Usually no. Tool doesn’t get built.
  • Now: “Is this tool worth 45 minutes?” Usually yes.

The people who figure this out will ship more, test ideas faster, and reserve their deep work for problems that actually need it.

Where Vibe Coding Excels

Section titled “Where Vibe Coding Excels”

Vibe coding is most valuable when:

  • The user is you (or your team, or your family)
  • The requirements are specific (your exact workflow, not generic best practices)
  • “Good enough” is good enough (it works for your situation, even with rough edges)
  • No one else would build this (too niche to be a product, too valuable to ignore)

The best vibe-coded tools aren’t apps you’d put on the App Store. They’re solutions to problems you’ve personally lived with—problems too specific for anyone else to solve.

Finding Problems Worth Solving

Section titled “Finding Problems Worth Solving”

Before you open any tool, spend time identifying problems that meet the criteria.

At Work

Section titled “At Work”

Where do you waste time on repetitive tasks? Manually formatting the same reports. Copy-pasting between systems. Creating similar documents over and over. Answering the same questions repeatedly.

Where do you lack visibility? Metrics that live in multiple places. Status updates you have to dig for. Information that should be at your fingertips but isn’t.

Where do existing tools fail you? They do 80% of what you need and miss the critical 20%. They require too many clicks for simple tasks. They weren’t built for your specific workflow.

In Your Personal Life

Section titled “In Your Personal Life”

What problems do you keep trying to solve manually? Tracking habits across different apps. Making decisions that have too many variables. Organizing information that doesn’t fit standard tools.

Where do you wish an app existed? Something for your specific hobby or interest. Tools for a unique life situation. The thing you’ve said “there should be an app for this” about.

Spend 30 minutes brainstorming. Write down every problem, no matter how small. The best projects often come from “minor” annoyances that add up.

The Three Domains

Section titled “The Three Domains”

Understanding which domain you’re in determines how you should approach the build.

Domain 1: Work Tools

Section titled “Domain 1: Work Tools”

Internal tools have the perfect vibe coding profile: known users (your colleagues), clear requirements (your workflow), and forgiving standards (it just needs to work).

Example: The Deck Generator

At Tenex, we create custom slide decks for every client meeting. Same structure, different content. Hours of work that felt like data entry.

What I built: A tool where you enter client details, select relevant case studies, and it generates a branded deck ready for the meeting.

The Prompt:

The engineering reality: The generated code is not how I would structure this. The state management is simplistic. There are no tests. The component organization is… fine.

Why it doesn’t matter: Three people use this tool. It saves each of them 2 hours a week. If it breaks, we’ll fix it. If it needs features, we’ll add them. The ROI was positive on day one.

When to vibe-code work tools:

  • You’ve done the task manually multiple times
  • The steps are consistent enough to systematize
  • A 70% solution would still save significant time
  • The alternative is “keep doing it manually forever”

When to build properly:

  • The tool handles sensitive data
  • Many people will depend on it
  • Errors have real consequences
  • It will need ongoing feature development

Domain 2: Product Work

Section titled “Domain 2: Product Work”

This is where vibe coding earns its keep for product teams. Ideas that used to require a sprint to test can be validated in a day.

Example: UX Prototyping

Before you commit engineering resources to a feature, you can vibe-code a working prototype to see if it even makes sense. Not a Figma mockup. Not a wireframe. A thing that works.

The Prompt:

In 2 hours, we had something we could put in front of users. We learned more from watching 5 people tap through it than we would have from 5 meetings debating the design.

When to vibe-code for product:

  • You need to test an idea before committing engineering resources
  • You want to align stakeholders on a direction
  • You’re exploring UX options
  • The artifact will be thrown away after validation

When to build properly:

  • The “prototype” might ship
  • You’re building an MVP that needs to evolve
  • Technical constraints matter for feasibility assessment
  • Performance or scale is part of what you’re validating

Domain 3: Personal Tools

Section titled “Domain 3: Personal Tools”

This is where vibe coding gets fun. Build exactly what you need, with zero compromise.

Example: The Habit Tracker (That Actually Works For You)

Most habit trackers are too generic. Build one around YOUR life.

Prompting Patterns That Work

Section titled “Prompting Patterns That Work”

Pattern 1: Structure First, Details Later

Section titled “Pattern 1: Structure First, Details Later”

Don’t try to describe everything in one prompt. Get the skeleton working, then add detail.

Bad:

Good:

Each prompt is testable. You verify it works before adding complexity.

Pattern 2: Describe the Outcome, Not the Implementation

Section titled “Pattern 2: Describe the Outcome, Not the Implementation”

The AI knows how to implement things. Your job is to describe what you need, not how to build it.

Bad:

Good:

Reserve implementation guidance for when the AI’s defaults aren’t what you want.

Pattern 3: Reference Visual Examples

Section titled “Pattern 3: Reference Visual Examples”

Instead of describing aesthetics from scratch, reference things the AI has seen.

Visual references communicate faster than adjectives.

Pattern 4: Specify What “Done” Looks Like

Section titled “Pattern 4: Specify What “Done” Looks Like”

Ambiguous prompts produce ambiguous results.

Bad:

Good:

Pattern 5: Guardrails on Changes

Section titled “Pattern 5: Guardrails on Changes”

Prevent the AI from “fixing” things you didn’t ask about.

Pattern 6: Include Your Constraints

Section titled “Pattern 6: Include Your Constraints”

Tell the AI about your actual situation.

When to Intervene

Section titled “When to Intervene”

Vibe coding doesn’t mean never looking at the code. It means being strategic about when you do.

Intervene when:

  • The AI is repeating the same mistake
  • Performance is noticeably bad
  • Security is a concern (auth, data handling)
  • You’ll need to maintain this code later
  • The structure prevents the next feature you need

Don’t intervene when:

  • The code is ugly but works
  • You could refactor it but don’t need to
  • The “right” way would take significantly longer
  • This is throwaway code anyway

The judgment call: Is fixing this code worth the time it takes?

Common Mistakes

Section titled “Common Mistakes”

  1. Starting Too Big: You want a “complete CRM” and end up with a mess. Start with one feature. Get it working. Expand.

  2. Not Testing Between Prompts: You stack five prompts, then discover something broke on prompt two. Now you’re debugging four prompts of changes. Test after each prompt.

  3. Over-Engineering Throwaway Code: You spend 3 hours refactoring a tool that 2 people will use. Ship the ugly version. Improve based on actual usage.

  4. Letting Prototypes Become Products: Your demo goes well. Stakeholders say “just ship this.” Resist. Prototypes have hidden fragility. Rebuild properly for production.

  5. Not Using Your Own Tools: Build → Use → Notice friction → Improve. The iteration happens in real life, not in prompts.

  6. Ignoring Mobile: Most personal tools are used on phones. If you don’t specify mobile-first, you’ll get a desktop app that’s painful on small screens.

Current Limitations

Section titled “Current Limitations”

  • Complex state management: Apps that require sophisticated state—real-time collaboration, complex undo/redo, offline-first sync—get difficult fast.

  • Performance at scale: Vibe-coded apps work fine for small user bases. At scale, you’ll hit problems the AI didn’t anticipate.

  • Security: AI-generated code can have vulnerabilities. Fine for internal tools; audit anything handling sensitive data.

  • Integration complexity: Simple API integrations work. Complex integrations with authentication, webhooks, and edge cases require engineering attention.

  • The “almost” trap: Getting to 90% is fast. The last 10%—polish, edge cases, mobile responsive—can take as long as the first 90%.

The Build Process

Section titled “The Build Process”

Step 1: Define the Problem (5 minutes)

Section titled “Step 1: Define the Problem (5 minutes)”

Write one sentence: “I need a tool that does X for Y audience because Z.”

If you can’t complete that sentence, you’re not ready to build.

Step 2: Decide: Vibe or Build? (2 minutes)

Section titled “Step 2: Decide: Vibe or Build? (2 minutes)”

Is this throwaway code (prototype, internal tool, personal utility) or something that needs engineering rigor?

Step 3: Describe “Done” (10 minutes)

Section titled “Step 3: Describe “Done” (10 minutes)”

What does the finished tool do? What does it NOT do? What’s the minimum viable version?

Step 4: Build the Skeleton (30 minutes)

Section titled “Step 4: Build the Skeleton (30 minutes)”

First prompt: get the core flow working with placeholder content. Verify navigation, layout, basic interactions.

Step 5: Add Features Incrementally

Section titled “Step 5: Add Features Incrementally”

One feature per prompt. Test after each. Checkpoint when things work.

Step 6: Scan the Code (10 minutes)

Section titled “Step 6: Scan the Code (10 minutes)”

Quick review: any obvious issues? Security concerns? Anything that would block future changes?

Step 7: Ship

Section titled “Step 7: Ship”

Deploy. Share. Use it. Improve based on reality.

Tools

Section titled “Tools”
  • Lovable: Best for consumer-style apps, beautiful defaults
  • Bolt.new: Good for quick prototypes
  • v0: Best for UI components you’ll integrate elsewhere
  • Replit: Best for projects you want to pick up yourself later
  • Cursor / Claude Code: When you need control or are in an existing codebase

TL;DR

Section titled “TL;DR”

Vibe coding is about matching effort to stakes. Some code deserves careful architecture. Some doesn’t. AI lets you build the latter in minutes.

Work tools: Automate the tedious 80%. The code quality bar is low because the user base is small and the alternative was “never building it.”

Product work: Test ideas fast. Prototypes are disposable—don’t let them become production code.

Personal tools: Build exactly what you need. You’re the only user, so polish doesn’t matter.

The process: Define the problem. Sketch the flow. Prompt for structure first. Add features one at a time. Actually use it. Iterate on real friction.

The mindset: Build for yourself. Start small. Ship ugly. Polish later—if ever.

The person who vibe codes generic apps gets frustrated. The person who vibe codes solutions to their specific problems gets tools they’ll use for years.

Build what only you would build. That’s vibe coding that’s actually useful.

Reference

Section titled “Reference”

Source: Thread by @ArmanHezarkhani

Tags:
  • Vibe Coding
  • AI Development
  • Productivity
  • Claude Code

vs-ai-agents Project Retrospective: Multi-Environment Deployment and RAG Context Retrieval Fix

Jan 21, 2026
Vibe Sparking

vs-ai-agents Project Retrospective: Multi-Environment Deployment and RAG Context Retrieval Fix

Section titled “vs-ai-agents Project Retrospective: Multi-Environment Deployment and RAG Context Retrieval Fix”

Project Background

Section titled “Project Background”

vs-ai-agents is a platform for creating custom AI Agents for entrepreneurs. This post documents key technical practices from 2026-01-21, covering multi-environment deployment optimization, environment configuration isolation, and debugging issues between Claude Agent SDK and WeChat Mini Program.

Core Challenges and Solutions

Section titled “Core Challenges and Solutions”

1. Multi-Environment Rsync Deployment Optimization

Section titled “1. Multi-Environment Rsync Deployment Optimization”

Problem: The original Makefile only supported a single remote path, making it impossible to distinguish between dev/uat/prod environments without manual configuration changes.

Solution:

Refactored the Makefile to introduce REMOTE_BASE_PATH and environment-specific path variables:

Additional Features:

CommandDescription
sync-dry-{env}Preview mode, no actual execution
sync-{env}Full sync with remote file deletion
sync-safe-{env}Safe sync, preserves remote files

Benefits:

  • One-command deployment to any environment
  • Preview mode reduces risk of misoperations
  • Safe sync mode protects production data

2. Missing TypeScript Type Declarations

Section titled “2. Missing TypeScript Type Declarations”

Problem: Running make build-uat caused the ops service compilation to fail:

Root Cause Analysis:

The ali-oss npm package doesn’t include TypeScript type definitions, and the project’s tsconfig has strict mode enabled (strict: true), disallowing implicit any types.

Solution:

Lessons Learned:

  1. Before adding third-party packages, check for corresponding @types/ packages
  2. For packages without type definitions, create a .d.ts declaration file as an alternative:

3. OSS Environment Configuration Isolation

Section titled “3. OSS Environment Configuration Isolation”

Problem: Files uploaded from the UAT miniapp were stored in the vs-ai-agents-entrepreneurs-dev bucket instead of the expected vs-ai-agents-entrepreneurs-uat bucket.

Investigation Process:

  1. Miniapp side: Checked .env.uat and src/utils/oss.ts, confirmed miniapp fetches OSS config from backend /api/v1/oss/sts
  2. Backend side: Checked ops/lib/oss.ts, found bucket name read from process.env.ALIYUN_OSS_BUCKET

Root Cause: The UAT environment’s .env file had ALIYUN_OSS_BUCKET still pointing to the dev bucket.

Solution:

Update UAT environment configuration:

Lessons Learned:

Environment configuration checklist:

Config Itemdevuatprod
API_BASEops-dev.vibesparking.comops-uat.vibesparking.comops.vibesparking.com
OSS_BUCKET…-dev…-uat…-prod
DATABASE_URLdb-devdb-uatdb-prod

4. Claude Agent SDK Attachment Context Retrieval Failure

Section titled “4. Claude Agent SDK Attachment Context Retrieval Failure”

Problem: After uploading an attachment and asking “summarize this file content”, the AI responded: “I need to view the file content before I can summarize it for you. Please provide one of the following…”

Symptom Analysis:

Backend logs showed:

Miniapp errors:

  1. Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'session_id')
  2. ReferenceError: TextDecoder is not defined

Root Cause Analysis:

  1. Backend issue: The logger variable was not defined in the knowledge context retrieval code, causing the entire retrieval process to fail
  2. Miniapp issue: WeChat miniprogram environment doesn’t support the native TextDecoder API, breaking the SSE stream parsing code

Problematic Code (Miniapp):

Solutions:

Backend Fix: Ensure logger is properly imported and initialized

Miniapp Fix: Use polyfill or alternative approach

Data Flow Comparison:

Lessons Learned

Section titled “Lessons Learned”

Multi-Environment Deployment

Section titled “Multi-Environment Deployment”
  1. Environment Isolation: dev/uat/prod should use independent directories, configs, and resources
  2. Progressive Deployment: dry-run → safe-sync → sync → deploy
  3. Configuration Checklists: Establish environment configuration comparison tables to avoid omissions

Cross-Platform Compatibility

Section titled “Cross-Platform Compatibility”
  1. API Differences: WeChat miniprogram environment lacks many browser APIs (TextDecoder, Fetch, etc.)
  2. Polyfill Strategy: Prefer officially recommended polyfills, then self-implementation
  3. Platform Detection: Runtime environment detection for dynamic implementation selection

Debugging Techniques

Section titled “Debugging Techniques”
  1. Log Tagging: [OSS_STS], [RAG_SEARCH], [CHAT_SEND] for easier issue location
  2. Full-Chain Tracing: Verify layer by layer from frontend request to backend processing
  3. Environment Isolation Verification: Immediately verify environment configuration correctness after deployment

Project Links

Section titled “Project Links”
  • Repository: vs-ai-agents (Private)
  • Tech Stack: FastAPI + Taro + Claude Agent SDK + Aliyun OSS

Through this day’s practice, we deeply experienced the complexity of multi-environment deployment and the challenges of cross-platform development. Sound environment configuration management and rigorous debugging methods are key to ensuring stable system operation.

Tags:
  • AI Agent
  • FastAPI
  • WeChat Mini Program
  • RAG
  • Docker
  • DevOps
  • Multi-Environment Deployment

UI-TOOLS: Open Source Creative Toolbox for Designers and Developers

Jan 20, 2026
Vibe Sparking

Introduction

Section titled “Introduction”

Open-source. Fast. Made for builders. — This is the promise of UI-TOOLS, a comprehensive creative toolbox developed by Naymur Rahman that empowers designers and developers to create stunning visual elements for modern web interfaces.

UI-TOOLS is a collection of specialized generators that help you create and customize shadows, SVG clip-paths, mesh gradients, background patterns, and color schemes — all in one place. Built with TypeScript, React, Next.js, and Tailwind CSS, it represents the modern approach to design engineering.

What is UI-TOOLS?

Section titled “What is UI-TOOLS?”

UI-TOOLS is an open-source project hosted at tools.ui-layouts.com that provides a suite of visual design generators. Whether you’re a designer looking for quick inspiration or a developer needing production-ready CSS, UI-TOOLS bridges the gap between design and implementation.

Key Features

Section titled “Key Features”

1. Shadow Generator

Section titled “1. Shadow Generator”

Create beautiful box shadows and text shadows with an intuitive visual interface. The shadow generator provides:

  • Real-time preview of shadow effects
  • Multiple shadow layers support
  • Copy-ready CSS, SCSS, and Tailwind code
  • Neumorphism and glassmorphism presets

2. SVG Clip-Path Generator

Section titled “2. SVG Clip-Path Generator”

Design custom clip-path shapes for images and containers:

  • Polygon and shape presets
  • Interactive path editing
  • Responsive-ready CSS output
  • Browser compatibility information

3. Mesh Gradients

Section titled “3. Mesh Gradients”

Generate stunning mesh gradient backgrounds:

  • React Three Fiber powered
  • Customizable color stops
  • Animation support
  • Export as CSS or React components

4. Background Snippets

Section titled “4. Background Snippets”

Browse and customize background patterns:

  • CSS-only patterns
  • Geometric and organic shapes
  • Scalable SVG backgrounds
  • One-click copy functionality

5. Color Lab

Section titled “5. Color Lab”

A comprehensive color toolkit featuring:

  • Shadcn theme generator
  • Color palette creator
  • HEX/RGBA/HSL converters
  • Color contrast checker for accessibility

Installation and Setup

Section titled “Installation and Setup”

Using the Web Tools

Section titled “Using the Web Tools”

Visit tools.ui-layouts.com to access all tools directly in your browser — no installation required.

Integrating Components Locally

Section titled “Integrating Components Locally”

To use UI-TOOLS components in your project:

Add the utility function to your utils.ts:

Add the media query hook for responsive components:

Available Tools

Section titled “Available Tools”
ToolURLDescription
Mesh Gradientstools.ui-layouts.com/mesh-gradientsCreate animated mesh gradient backgrounds
Shadowstools.ui-layouts.com/shadowsGenerate box and text shadows
SVG Clip-Pathtools.ui-layouts.com/clip-pathsDesign custom clip-path shapes
BG Snippetstools.ui-layouts.com/background-snippetsBrowse background patterns
Color Labtools.ui-layouts.com/color-labColor tools and theme generation

Recommended Use Cases

Section titled “Recommended Use Cases”

1. Landing Page Design

Section titled “1. Landing Page Design”

Quickly prototype and implement visual effects:

2. Dashboard Components

Section titled “2. Dashboard Components”

Create consistent shadow depths for cards and panels:

  • Level 0: Flat surfaces
  • Level 1: Elevated cards
  • Level 2: Dropdowns and popovers
  • Level 3: Modals and dialogs

3. Image Effects

Section titled “3. Image Effects”

Apply creative clip-paths for unique image presentations:

4. Accessibility Compliance

Section titled “4. Accessibility Compliance”

Use the Color Lab contrast checker to ensure WCAG compliance:

  • WCAG AA: 4.5:1 for normal text
  • WCAG AAA: 7:1 for normal text

5. Design Systems

Section titled “5. Design Systems”

Export and standardize design tokens across your organization.

Best Practices

Section titled “Best Practices”

1. Performance Considerations

Section titled “1. Performance Considerations”
  • Use CSS transforms instead of box-shadow for animated elements
  • Prefer will-change sparingly for animated shadows
  • Consider using SVG patterns for scalable backgrounds

2. Accessibility

Section titled “2. Accessibility”
  • Always test color contrast ratios
  • Provide fallbacks for clip-path in unsupported browsers
  • Ensure shadows don’t reduce text readability

3. Browser Support

Section titled “3. Browser Support”

4. Responsive Design

Section titled “4. Responsive Design”

Use the media query hook to adapt effects:

5. Design Consistency

Section titled “5. Design Consistency”

Create a shadow scale in your Tailwind config:

Comparison with Similar Tools

Section titled “Comparison with Similar Tools”
FeatureUI-TOOLSFFFuelCSS PortalProCSSGen
Shadow Generator✅✅✅✅
Clip-Path Editor✅❌✅✅
Mesh Gradients✅✅❌❌
Background Patterns✅✅❌✅
Color Tools✅✅❌✅
React Export✅❌❌✅
Open Source✅❌❌❌
Tailwind Support✅Partial❌✅

Technical Stack

Section titled “Technical Stack”

UI-TOOLS is built with modern web technologies:

  • Framework: Next.js 14 with App Router
  • Language: TypeScript (99.5%)
  • Styling: Tailwind CSS
  • Animations: Framer Motion
  • 3D Graphics: React Three Fiber (for mesh gradients)
  • UI Components: Shadcn UI
  • Deployment: Vercel

Project Structure

Section titled “Project Structure”

Precautions and Limitations

Section titled “Precautions and Limitations”

1. Browser Compatibility

Section titled “1. Browser Compatibility”

Some features may not work in older browsers:

  • clip-path requires modern browsers
  • Mesh gradients need WebGL support
  • Always provide fallbacks

2. Performance Impact

Section titled “2. Performance Impact”

Multiple box-shadows can affect rendering performance:

  • Limit shadow layers to 2-3
  • Use transform: translateZ() for GPU acceleration
  • Test on mobile devices

3. Learning Curve

Section titled “3. Learning Curve”

While the tools are intuitive, understanding:

  • CSS shadow syntax
  • Clip-path coordinate systems
  • Color theory
  • Accessibility standards

…will improve your results.

Troubleshooting

Section titled “Troubleshooting”

Issue: Shadows appear cut off

Section titled “Issue: Shadows appear cut off”

Solution: Add padding to container:

Issue: Clip-path not working

Section titled “Issue: Clip-path not working”

Solution: Check browser support and add prefixes:

Issue: Mesh gradient not rendering

Section titled “Issue: Mesh gradient not rendering”

Solution: Ensure WebGL is enabled and the browser supports React Three Fiber.

Issue: Color contrast warnings

Section titled “Issue: Color contrast warnings”

Solution: Use the Color Lab to find compliant color combinations while maintaining your design intent.

FAQ

Section titled “FAQ”

Q: Is UI-TOOLS free to use?

A: Yes, UI-TOOLS is completely open-source and free. You can use the web tools or self-host the code.

Q: Can I contribute to UI-TOOLS?

A: Absolutely! The project is on GitHub at github.com/ui-layouts/ui-tools. Contributions are welcome.

Q: Do I need to install anything to use the tools?

A: No, all tools are available at tools.ui-layouts.com. Installation is only needed if you want to integrate components into your project.

Q: What’s the difference between using the web tools vs. integrating locally?

A: The web tools are for quick prototyping and code generation. Local integration gives you full customization and React component access.

Q: Are there plans for additional tools?

A: Yes, the project is actively developed. Check the GitHub issues and roadmap for upcoming features.

Reference Links

Section titled “Reference Links”
  • Official Website
  • GitHub Repository
  • DeepWiki Documentation
  • Author: Naymur Rahman
  • Twitter: @naymur_dev
  • LinkedIn

Conclusion

Section titled “Conclusion”

UI-TOOLS represents a valuable addition to any web developer’s toolkit. By combining multiple design generators into one cohesive platform, it streamlines the workflow between design and implementation.

Whether you’re creating landing pages, building design systems, or simply need quick CSS snippets, UI-TOOLS provides the speed and flexibility that modern development demands. The fact that it’s open-source means you can learn from the code, customize it for your needs, and contribute back to the community.

For designers, it offers quick inspiration and production-ready code. For developers, it bridges the gap between design intent and technical implementation. And for everyone, it’s free, fast, and continuously improving.

Published: January 20, 2026

Tags:
  • ui-tools
  • design
  • developer-tools
  • open-source
  • tailwindcss
  • react

Agent Skills: One Repo to Manage All AI Agent Skills with Automatic Sync

Jan 20, 2026
Vibe Sparking

Introduction

Section titled “Introduction”

If you use multiple AI tools like Claude Code, Codex CLI, and ChatGPT simultaneously, you’ve probably run into this annoyance:

Each tool has its own “skills” directory. The same skill needs to be maintained in multiple places. One edit means manually copying to multiple locations, and forgetting to sync leads to version inconsistencies.

regenrek/agent-skills solves this problem - one repo to manage all AI Agent skills, automatically synced to each tool.

Key Highlights

Section titled “Key Highlights”

Single Source of Truth

Section titled “Single Source of Truth”

Advantages:

  • Write each skill only once
  • Git version control
  • Fork to customize

Automatic Sync Mechanism

Section titled “Automatic Sync Mechanism”
TriggerWhen It Syncs
Git hooksAfter commit / pull / checkout
File watcherImmediately on save

Detailed Walkthrough

Section titled “Detailed Walkthrough”

1. Basic Structure

Section titled “1. Basic Structure”

Required file: Each skill must contain SKILL.md

Getting started:

2. Sync Script

Section titled “2. Sync Script”

Core command: sync-skills.sh

rsync parameters:

ParameterPurpose
-aPreserve structure and timestamps
-LFollow symlinks (copy real files)
--deleteRemove files in target not present in source

Sync targets:

  • ~/.claude/skills/ - Claude Code
  • ~/.codex/skills/ - Codex CLI
  • Extensible to other tools

3. Git Hooks Automation

Section titled “3. Git Hooks Automation”

Config location: .githooks/

Enable hooks:

Auto-sync triggers:

  • git commit - Sync after commit
  • git pull / git merge - Sync after merge
  • git checkout - Sync after branch switch

4. File Watcher (Optional)

Section titled “4. File Watcher (Optional)”

watchexec (cross-platform):

entr (minimal):

Available Skills

Section titled “Available Skills”

agent-skills includes several practical skills:

SkillDescription
codex-sandboxCreate isolated git sandboxes for Codex CLI
create-new-static-websiteCreate new projects from astro-website-starter
debug-lldbCapture and analyze thread backtraces with LLDB/GDB
gh-repo-bootstrapCreate GitHub repos and init local projects with gh CLI
git-safe-workflowSafe check, stage, and commit AI agent changes
gitwhatConcise git workspace snapshot
go-local-healthRun local Go health checks
redesign-my-landingpageBuild high-conversion landing pages
shellckRun shellcheck on shell scripts

Recommended Scenarios and Best Practices

Section titled “Recommended Scenarios and Best Practices”

Workflow Selection

Section titled “Workflow Selection”
ScenarioRecommendation
Regular developmentGit hooks - set and forget
Rapid iterationFile watcher - see changes in real-time
Best of bothGit hooks + watcher when needed

Creating Custom Skills

Section titled “Creating Custom Skills”

Steps:

Team Collaboration

Section titled “Team Collaboration”

Scenario: Shared team skill library

Comparison with Alternatives

Section titled “Comparison with Alternatives”
Aspectagent-skillsManual CopyTool-Specific Maintenance
Maintenance CostEdit onceCopy everywhereEdit everywhere
Version ControlGit semanticChaoticIndependent
Team CollaborationPR/MergeDifficultNo collaboration
Auto SyncHooks/WatcherManualNone
Cross-Tool CompatUnified formatDependsInconsistent

Important Notes and FAQ

Section titled “Important Notes and FAQ”

Limitations

Section titled “Limitations”

Q: Which AI tools are supported?

A: Currently natively supported:

  • Claude Code (~/.claude/skills/)
  • Codex CLI (~/.codex/skills/)

Extend to other tools by modifying sync-skills.sh.

Q: Is the skill format standardized?

A: SKILL.md is the de facto standard:

  • Agreed upon by AI tool developers
  • Contains skill description, parameters, usage examples
  • Human-readable + AI-parseable

Q: How to ensure skill quality?

A:

  • Review skill ratings before forking
  • Test before merging to main branch
  • Establish code review processes within teams

Q: Does file watching consume significant resources?

A:

  • Both watchexec and entr are lightweight
  • Only triggers sync on file changes
  • Can be started/stopped on demand

References

Section titled “References”
  • GitHub: regenrek/agent-skills
  • Author X/Twitter: @kregenrek
  • Author Bluesky: @kevinkern.dev
  • Related Course: Ultimate Cursor Course
  • Related Project: codefetch - Code to Markdown tool

Summary

Section titled “Summary”

agent-skills is a simple yet powerful solution:

  1. Single repo - All skills managed centrally
  2. Auto sync - Git hooks + file watcher
  3. Cross-tool compatibility - Claude, Codex, ChatGPT
  4. Version control - Full Git history
  5. Team collaboration - Fork, PR, Merge

Getting started:

Core philosophy: One repo. All your AI agents. Always in sync.

Questions or suggestions? Join the discussion on GitHub Issues!

Tags:
  • agent-skills
  • AI Development
  • Skill Sync
  • Git-hooks
  • Claude-Code
  • Developer Tools
  • Automation

Building AI Digital Twins for Entrepreneurs: Technical Deep Dive into vs-ai-agents

Jan 20, 2026
Vibe Sparking

Building AI Digital Twins for Entrepreneurs: Technical Deep Dive into vs-ai-agents

Section titled “Building AI Digital Twins for Entrepreneurs: Technical Deep Dive into vs-ai-agents”

Project Overview

Section titled “Project Overview”

vs-ai-agents is a platform designed to create custom AI Agent personas for entrepreneurs, helping them build personal IP characters. By integrating large language models, it enables conversation history memory, cross-session memory, and natural dialogue with AI digital twins that “think like the real person.”

Technology Stack

Section titled “Technology Stack”
ModuleTechnology
User AppWeChat Mini Program (Taro + Taroify + MobX)
Ops DashboardReact + Next.js + Ant Design
Backend APIFastAPI + SQLAlchemy (asyncio)
StoragePostgreSQL + pgvector + Redis
AI ModelsClaude Agent SDK + DeepSeek
DeploymentDocker Compose + Kamal + Aliyun

Core Challenges and Solutions

Section titled “Core Challenges and Solutions”

1. User Role Unification: From Visitor/Entrepreneur to User

Section titled “1. User Role Unification: From Visitor/Entrepreneur to User”

Problem: The initial design distinguished between “visitor” and “entrepreneur” roles, leading to complex data models and difficult code maintenance.

Solution:

Merged entrepreneurs and visitors tables into a unified users table with consistent foreign key naming:

Benefits:

  • Simplified data model and business logic
  • Reduced code duplication
  • Improved system maintainability

2. Memory Type Unification: Single Source of Truth Architecture

Section titled “2. Memory Type Unification: Single Source of Truth Architecture”

Problem: Memory type definitions were scattered across three locations (FastAPI backend, Claude service, Mini Program frontend), causing inconsistencies and maintenance difficulties.

Solution:

Established a single source of truth architecture with all memory type definitions unified in FastAPI Schema:

Claude service dynamically generates extraction instructions, and the Mini Program uses explicit comments pointing to the source.

3. Enhanced Chat Context Management

Section titled “3. Enhanced Chat Context Management”

Problem: The original implementation had a hardcoded 20-message history limit, no token budget awareness, and no cross-session context retrieval capability.

Solution:

Implemented a three-tier context management system:

Token Budget Allocation:

Cross-Session Context Retrieval: Uses PostgreSQL Full-Text Search to retrieve semantically relevant conversation snippets from other sessions.

4. Memory Source Tracking and Deep-Link Navigation

Section titled “4. Memory Source Tracking and Deep-Link Navigation”

Problem: Users couldn’t know where memories came from and couldn’t quickly navigate to the original conversation.

Solution:

  1. Database Extension: Added source_type enum and search_vector (TSVECTOR) column
  2. Full-Text Search: PostgreSQL tsvector + GIN index
  3. Deep-Link Navigation: Implemented cross-TabBar page navigation through MobX Store

5. Attachment Knowledge Context Retrieval Fix

Section titled “5. Attachment Knowledge Context Retrieval Fix”

Problem: After users uploaded attachments, AI responded with “I need you to provide the document” instead of automatically retrieving attachment content.

Root Cause Analysis:

RAG search relies on vector similarity (threshold ≥ 0.5). When users send generic requests like “summarize the article content,” the similarity to specific document content was too low, causing empty results.

Solution:

Added a direct attachment retrieval method that bypasses similarity search:

Fixed Data Flow:

Deployment and Operations

Section titled “Deployment and Operations”

Kamal Zero-Downtime Deployment

Section titled “Kamal Zero-Downtime Deployment”

Using Kamal for containerized zero-downtime deployment:

Multi-Environment Rsync Isolation

Section titled “Multi-Environment Rsync Isolation”

Makefile commands:

  • make sync-dev / make sync-uat / make sync-prod
  • make deploy-dev / make deploy-uat / make deploy-prod

Lessons Learned

Section titled “Lessons Learned”

Architecture Design

Section titled “Architecture Design”
  1. Single Source of Truth Principle: Cross-language/cross-module shared definitions should have a clear single source
  2. Progressive Unification: Refactor promptly when redundancy is discovered to avoid tech debt accumulation
  3. Configuration over Hardcoding: Token budgets, history message counts, and similar parameters should be configurable

RAG Best Practices

Section titled “RAG Best Practices”
  1. Vector Similarity Has Limitations: Generic queries may not match specific content
  2. Direct Retrieval as Supplement: When users explicitly specify documents, bypass similarity search
  3. Multi-Strategy Combination: Similarity search + direct retrieval + full-text search

Operations Best Practices

Section titled “Operations Best Practices”
  1. Environment Isolation: dev/uat/prod use separate directories and configurations
  2. Progressive Deployment: First dry-run, then safe-sync, finally deploy
  3. Log Tagging: [RAG_SEARCH], [RAG_DIRECT], [CHAT_SEND] for easier debugging

Project Links

Section titled “Project Links”
  • Repository: vs-ai-agents (private)
  • Tech Stack Documentation: FastAPI + Taro + Claude Agent SDK

Through this project, we deeply realized that AI Agent application development is not just about calling LLM APIs—it’s more about building reliable context management, memory systems, and deployment/operations infrastructure. We hope these experiences can provide references for other AI Agent developers.

Tags:
  • AI Agent
  • FastAPI
  • WeChat Mini Program
  • RAG
  • Docker
  • Kamal
  • Full Stack Development

Codex Skills: AI Agent Skill Library for Smarter Code Assistants

Jan 20, 2026
Vibe Sparking

Codex Skills: AI Agent Skill Library for Smarter Code Assistants

Section titled “Codex Skills: AI Agent Skill Library for Smarter Code Assistants”

Introduction

Section titled “Introduction”

am-will/codex-skills is a curated collection of open-source AI Agent skills designed to extend the capabilities of AI code assistants like OpenAI Codex and Claude Code.

Key Highlights

Section titled “Key Highlights”
  1. Unified Skill Standard: Follows the Agent Skills Open Standard - write once, use everywhere
  2. Rich Pre-built Skills: Covers browser automation, document querying, frontend best practices, and more
  3. Easy Installation: Supports one-click installation via npx globally or to specific projects
  4. Modular Design: Each skill is independently maintained and can be installed as needed

Links

Section titled “Links”
  • GitHub: am-will/codex-skills
  • Chinese Version: 中文版本
Tags:
  • AI Development
  • Codex
  • Claude Code
  • Agent Skills
  • MCP
